eli3: Multiple linear regression extends simple regression to several predictors: the outcome is modeled as a linear combination of all predictors plus an error term. The OLS estimator finds all coefficients simultaneously by minimizing the total squared residual across all data points.

Each coefficient gives the partial effect of its predictor on the outcome while holding all other predictors constant. This is the key advantage over simple regression: it controls for confounding. Omitting an important predictor that correlates with an included one causes omitted variable bias — the included predictor's coefficient absorbs the effect of the missing one.

Multicollinearity — strong correlation among predictors — inflates the variance of coefficient estimates, making them unstable and hard to interpret. The Variance Inflation Factor for each predictor measures how much its variance is inflated relative to the case where it is uncorrelated with the others; values above ten signal a serious problem. Prediction accuracy is largely unaffected.

whenWhy.title: Controlling for confounders in observational studies
whenWhy.body: In observational data, a predictor of interest X₁ may be correlated with other variables X₂,…,Xₚ that also affect Y. Including X₂,…,Xₚ in the regression adjusts for these confounders and isolates the partial effect of X₁. Example: estimating the effect of education on wages while controlling for experience, industry, and geography. This is the primary use of multiple regression in econometrics and epidemiology.

whenWhy.title: Model selection: which predictors to include?
whenWhy.body: Including irrelevant predictors increases variance (lower power) but doesn't bias estimates. Omitting relevant predictors that are correlated with included ones biases estimates. Selection methods: forward stepwise, backward elimination, LASSO (data-driven). AIC = n·log(SS_res/n) + 2p and BIC = n·log(SS_res/n) + p·log(n) penalize complexity; lower is better. R² increases with every added predictor — use adjusted R² or cross-validated RMSE instead.

whenWhy.title: Testing groups of predictors: partial F-test
whenWhy.body: To test whether a subset of q predictors (H₀: β_{p−q+1} = … = βₚ = 0) contributes beyond the reduced model, use the partial F-test: F = [(SS_res(reduced) − SS_res(full))/q] / [SS_res(full)/(n−p−1)] ~ F(q, n−p−1) under H₀. This is more powerful than testing each coefficient individually with multiple t-tests.

deepDiveIntro: The model is written as outcome equals design matrix times coefficient vector plus error, where the design matrix includes a column of ones for the intercept. The OLS estimator requires the product of the design matrix with its transpose to be invertible — which fails if any predictor is a perfect linear combination of others or if there are more predictors than observations. Fitted values come from multiplying the data by the hat matrix, which projects the outcome onto the column space of the predictors. The residual sum of squares divided by the degrees of freedom is an unbiased estimator of the error variance.

The Gauss-Markov theorem says OLS is the best linear unbiased estimator under linearity, constant error variance, and zero-mean errors conditional on the predictors. Adding a normality assumption gives exact t-tests for individual coefficients and an F-test for all non-intercept coefficients simultaneously.

deepDive.heading: OLS in Matrix Form
deepDive.body: In matrix form, multiple regression estimates all coefficients at once by projecting the outcome onto the column space spanned by the predictors. This viewpoint makes it easier to understand leverage, fitted values, and uncertainty in the coefficient estimates. It also explains why linear algebra issues such as rank deficiency and collinearity matter so much in regression.

deepDive.heading: Omitted Variable Bias
deepDive.body: Leaving out a relevant predictor can distort the coefficients on variables that remain in the model. The bias appears when the omitted variable both affects the outcome and is correlated with an included predictor. This is why regression coefficients should be interpreted as conditional effects only within a thoughtfully specified model.

deepDive.heading: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)
deepDive.body: Variance inflation factors quantify how much collinearity among predictors weakens coefficient precision. When one predictor is largely explained by the others, its unique contribution becomes hard to isolate and its standard error grows. This hurts interpretation and hypothesis testing even when predictions remain fairly accurate.

deepDive.heading: Influential Observations: Cook's Distance
deepDive.body: Some observations matter much more than others because they combine large residuals with unusual predictor values. Cook's distance summarizes how much the fitted model would change if one observation were removed. It is a practical way to identify cases that deserve closer inspection before drawing substantive conclusions.

takeaways: β̂ = (X'X)⁻¹X'y; each βⱼ is the partial effect of Xⱼ holding other predictors constant. [1]
Omitted variable bias: omitting Z correlated with X biases β̂ unless Cov(X,Z) = 0 or γ = 0. [1]
VIFⱼ = 1/(1−Rⱼ²); VIF > 10 indicates severe multicollinearity and inflated SE(β̂ⱼ). [1]
Partial F-test for nested models: F = [(SS_res(R) − SS_res(F))/q] / [SS_res(F)/(n−p−1)]. [1]
AIC/BIC for model selection; adjusted R² (penalizes added predictors) over plain R². [1]

edgeCases.q: What if X'X is singular (perfect multicollinearity)?
edgeCases.a: (X'X)⁻¹ does not exist when X has perfectly collinear columns (e.g., X₃ = X₁ + X₂, or a dummy variable trap). The OLS solution is not unique — infinitely many β values minimize SS_res. Software typically drops one column or adds a small ridge penalty. In practice, use Ridge regression or check for linearly dependent columns (e.g., including all K dummies for a K-category variable without dropping one). [1]

edgeCases.q: Does standardizing predictors affect OLS estimates?
edgeCases.a: Standardizing (z-scoring) predictors makes coefficients comparable in magnitude: β̂ⱼ_std = β̂ⱼ · SD(Xⱼ)/SD(Y) is the expected change in Y (in SDs) per 1-SD change in Xⱼ. This doesn't change model fit (R², F-test, residuals) or significance of t-tests but makes multicollinearity-induced VIFs slightly smaller for correlated predictors. Standardization does not fix fundamental multicollinearity — the VIF formula is scale-invariant. [1]

sources.label: Montgomery, Peck & Vining — Introduction to Linear Regression Analysis (5th ed.), Ch. 3–10
